title: OperationalDNA
description:
A complete Operational DNA document for a business. Operational DNA expresses pure business logic — what the business does, independent of UI, API, or deployment technology. Modeled around the Actor > Action > Resource triad.

Resources are the only noun collection. Roles, Users, and Groups are all Resources — distinguished by how they are referenced (structural typing), not by a declared type field. A Resource that is referenced from `Membership.role` or `Task.actor` is acting as a Role; a Resource with `memberships[]` is acting as a User; a Resource referenced from `Membership.in` or another Resource's `scope` is acting as a Group.

domain.description: The root domain, containing the full nested hierarchy of subdomains and resources.

operations.description: All Resource.Action operation pairs defined in this domain — the atomic units of business activity.

triggers.description: What initiates each Operation or Process — user actions, schedules, webhooks, chained operations, or signals.

rules.description: Constraints on operations — who may perform them (access rules) and what conditions must be satisfied (condition rules). Condition rules are also referenced by Step.conditions[] for compositional gating.

outcomes.description: State changes and side effects that result from each operation.

equations.description: Named, technology-agnostic computations that cannot be expressed as declarative Rules or Outcomes. Implemented concretely by Scripts in Technical DNA.

signals.description: Named domain events published after an Operation executes. Signals cross domain boundaries and carry typed payload contracts.

relationships.description: Named, directed connections between Resources — formalizes the semantic link a reference Attribute implies, adding cardinality.

tasks.description: Atomic reusable units of business activity: an Actor (typically a Resource acting as a Role) performing exactly one Operation.

processes.description: Standard Operating Procedures — named, owned DAGs of Steps that accomplish business goals. Each Process declares an explicit `startStep` (Amazon-States-Language convention).
